intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: A personal health dashboard works better with warm, earthy color schemes that feel supportive and lived-in rather than clinical or cold
facade.label: Warm amber tones
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward inviting, organic color palettes that reduce tracking fatigue and create a sense of comfort around health data
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers crisp, clinical, or cool-toned interfaces that feel objective and scientifically trustworthy for health information

facade.hypothesis: Users building health experiments prefer a supportive voice that celebrates small wins and normalizes setbacks, rather than clinical or detached language
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — friendly and encouraging vs. neutral and clinical
facade.label: Warm, encouraging tone
facade.acceptImplies: Copy should use conversational language, positive reinforcement, and human-centered framing ('You're tracking...', 'Great job logging...', celebration moments)
facade.rejectImplies: Voice should remain objective, data-focused, and measured — letting numbers speak for themselves without emotional scaffolding

evidence.hypothesis: A personal health dashboard works better with warm, earthy color schemes that feel supportive and lived-in rather than clinical or cold
evidence.implication: User gravitates toward inviting, organic color palettes that reduce tracking fatigue and create a sense of comfort around health data

facade.hypothesis: Health dashboards benefit from animated transitions that ease data entry and result visibility rather than instant state changes
facade.axisTargeted: Motion responsiveness—fluid animated transitions vs. instant/snappy state updates
facade.label: Smooth slide-in
facade.acceptImplies: User expects data fields, metric cards, and insights to glide into view, stagger, or animate on load; transitions feel like part of the storytelling
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers snappy, direct interactions with minimal motion; state changes should feel immediate and responsive to input, not delayed by choreography

facade.hypothesis: A persistent left sidebar for experiment navigation paired with card-based layout for individual health metrics will feel organized without overwhelming the eye.
facade.axisTargeted: Layout structure: sidebar vs tabs / cards vs lists
facade.label: Sidebar + cards
facade.acceptImplies: User values vertical information hierarchy and persistent navigation anchors; they want experiments always visible and clickable
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers tab-based or full-width layouts; they find sidebars claustrophobic or want maximum content space

facade.hypothesis: Users prioritize one key metric per experiment (e.g., 'Sleep: 7.2 hrs') over narrative context or trend explanation
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data (single prominent number) vs narrative framing (number + story)
facade.label: Big number, small story
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard favors bold, isolated metrics—large typography, minimal surrounding text. Good for at-a-glance status.
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard wraps numbers in context—each metric includes why it matters, what changed, what's next. Good for reflection and decision-making.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard works best with clean, sparse data visualization—thin lines, plenty of white space, no decorative elements
facade.label: Minimal line graphs
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward uncluttered charts; likely prefers data-forward design over visual warmth or metaphor
facade.rejectImplies: User may want more visual richness, color-coding, or contextual imagery to make tracking feel less clinical

facade.hypothesis: A personal health dashboard feels less overwhelming when data visualization is sparse and layouts have generous whitespace, allowing users to process one metric at a time rather than scanning a dense dashboard
facade.axisTargeted: Calm vs energetic — whether the interface feels meditative and uncluttered or information-rich and stimulating
facade.label: Minimal graphs, maximum breathing room
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers contemplative, slow interfaces that reduce cognitive load; likely wants to check in on health data as a reflective ritual rather than constant monitoring
facade.rejectImplies: User wants comprehensive information density and multiple data points visible at once; may prefer quick-scan dashboards that show progress across all experiments simultaneously
